evidence: CSWP 39 §3 cites ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as one of the three mandatory-to-implement post-quantum algorithms for systems transitioning away from classical public-key cryptography.
CSWP 39 §3 cites ML-DSA (FIPS 204) as one of the three mandatory-to-implement post-quantum algorithms alongside ML-KEM and SLH-DSA.
CSWP 39 §3 cites SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) as the stateless hash-based signature algorithm required for cryptographic agility and backup-algorithm resilience.

CSWP 39 §4 applies the SP 800-131A Rev 3 transition framework as the normative deprecation schedule for classical algorithm migration; CSWP 39 guidance is a policy application of that broader transition framework.
CSWP 39 §2 references IR 8413 (Third Round Status Report) as the baseline selection rationale for the three standardized PQC algorithms; IR 8413 documents the selection decisions CSWP 39 builds upon.

CSWP 39 §5 references TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446) as the primary network protocol context for PQC migration; hybrid key exchange via ML-KEM is the main deployment path for CSWP 39 recommendations in transport security.

CSWP 39 §5 references KEM integration in CMS (RFC 9629) as the messaging and certificate infrastructure path for PQC migration; organizations implementing CSWP 39 must address CMS-based key transport as well as TLS.
CNSA 2.0 §2.1 mandates ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the required KEM algorithm for NSS; CNSA 2.0 is an implementation mandate that is a subset of the broader FIPS 203 standard.
CNSA 2.0 §2.2 mandates ML-DSA (FIPS 204) as the required digital signature algorithm for NSS; CNSA 2.0 is an implementation mandate that is a subset of the broader FIPS 204 standard.
CNSA 2.0 §2.2 mandates SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) as an approved stateless hash-based alternative signature algorithm for NSS; CNSA 2.0 guidance is a subset of the broader FIPS 205 standard.

SP 800-131A Rev 3 provides the normative algorithm deprecation and key-length schedule that CSWP 39 applies as a migration policy; SP 800-131A is the broader transition framework of which CSWP 39 guidance is a policy application.

ASD ISM (December 2024) §6 references the NIST PQC standardization programme and CSWP 39 cryptographic agility guidance as the foundation for ASD algorithm migration recommendations; Five Eyes alignment is explicit.
ASD ISM (December 2024) §6.2 lists ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as an approved post-quantum key encapsulation algorithm for Australian government systems.
NCSC Preparing for Quantum-Safe Cryptography references NIST PQC standards and cryptographic agility principles consistent with CSWP 39; Five Eyes coordination is referenced throughout.
NCSC Preparing for Quantum-Safe Cryptography endorses ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as the primary post-quantum KEM algorithm recommended for UK organisations transitioning away from RSA/ECC.
Canada TBS SPIN 2025-01 §3 references NIST cryptographic agility guidance (CSWP 39) as a key input to the GC PQC migration roadmap; Canadian policy mirrors NIST migration framework.
Canada TBS SPIN 2025-01 §4 endorses ML-KEM (FIPS 203) as an approved algorithm for Government of Canada systems transitioning to post-quantum cryptography.
NIST published ML-KEM (FIPS 203) in August 2024 as one of three inaugural post-quantum cryptography standards; this event marks the formal standardization milestone for the algorithm.
NIST published ML-DSA (FIPS 204) in August 2024 as one of three inaugural post-quantum cryptography standards; this event marks the formal standardization milestone for the algorithm.
NIST published SLH-DSA (FIPS 205) in August 2024 as one of three inaugural post-quantum cryptography standards; this event marks the formal standardization milestone for the algorithm.
NSA CNSA 2.0 mandates that new NSS acquisitions must include PQC algorithm support; the NSS Acquisitions deadline is a direct enforcement timeline for CNSA 2.0 compliance.

CSWP 39 was published concurrently with FIPS 203/204/205 as the migration guidance document; the standards finalization event is the prerequisite that enabled CSWP 39 to reference specific algorithm identifiers.
